skill.name: api-security-best-practices
skill.description: Guidance for designing and reviewing secure APIs with authentication, authorization, input validation, rate limiting, and OWASP API controls.
skill.summary: Secure API design guidance for authentication, authorization, validation, rate limiting, and OWASP-aligned reviews.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are inside Markdown examples and security guidance, not executable skill code. The `process.env` references, URLs, and backtick markers are instructional examples or resource links. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, hidden execution, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution is caused by escaped Markdown code fences and JavaScript template string examples. The skill contains documentation examples, not runnable hooks or scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are inside Markdown examples or related-skill references. I found no executable wrapper, script file, or instruction to run shell commands.

low_findings.title: Credential Access Findings Are Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: The `process.env` references show how application code should load JWT and Redis configuration. They do not read local environment files or transmit secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references appear in illustrative Node.js API snippets. There is no network call or file operation connected to these values.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The network findings point to OWASP, standards, framework documentation, and security checklist links. The skill does not fetch these URLs or send data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged URLs are Markdown links in an Additional Resources section. No code invokes HTTP clients or exfiltrates data.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Reconnaissance Findings Are Pattern Matches
low_findings.description: The hidden-file and reconnaissance signals come from validation regex, error-response examples, ORM calls, and checklist text. They are not filesystem access or host reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is application security training content. I found no commands that list files, inspect the host, or access hidden files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are not supported by the cited lines. The file recommends bcrypt, strong JWT secrets, HTTPS, HSTS, and secure headers.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak primitives. The surrounding guidance promotes stronger controls.

low_findings.title: Heuristic Dangerous Combination Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The combined code execution, network, and credential-access heuristic is explained by documentation examples appearing in one Markdown file. No malicious coordination or exfiltration intent was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious categories occur in separate instructional examples and resource links. The skill has no executable assets beyond the Markdown content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Intentionally Vulnerable Educational Snippets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes unsafe examples such as string-built SQL, hardcoded JWT secrets, missing authorization checks, and verbose errors. Each is labeled as bad or paired with a safer replacement.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These snippets are semantically risky if copied without context, but the file clearly marks them as anti-patterns and shows corrected versions.

content.value_statement: APIs often fail through weak authentication, unsafe input handling, and missing rate limits. This skill gives practical patterns for designing, reviewing, and hardening API endpoints.
actual_capabilities: Explains secure authentication flows for JWT, refresh tokens, and protected routes.
Shows input validation and SQL injection prevention patterns for Node.js APIs.
Provides rate limiting, throttling, and DDoS protection guidance for Express APIs.
Covers secure error handling, CORS, headers, and sensitive data handling.
Maps reviews to common OWASP API Security Top 10 risk areas.
limitations: Does not run automated security scanners or verify a live API implementation.
Examples focus mainly on JavaScript, Express, Prisma, Redis, and Zod patterns.
Requires adaptation for other frameworks, compliance regimes, and threat models.
Does not replace professional penetration testing or formal security review.

use_cases.title: Design a New Secure Endpoint
use_cases.description: Plan authentication, validation, authorization, and rate limits before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review Existing API Controls
use_cases.description: Check endpoints for common gaps such as weak object authorization or verbose errors.

use_cases.title: Prepare for an API Audit
use_cases.description: Create a concise checklist aligned to common API security control areas.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Endpoint
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single route.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this API endpoint for authentication, authorization, input validation, rate limiting, and error handling gaps. Explain the highest-risk issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Design Secure Authentication
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate design for login and session flows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a secure JWT authentication flow for my API. Include token lifetime, refresh token storage, revocation, error responses, and secret handling.

prompt_templates.title: Build an API Hardening Checklist
prompt_templates.scenario: Team checklist for a service release.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release checklist for this API service covering OWASP API risks, validation, CORS, headers, logging, monitoring, and rate limiting.

prompt_templates.title: Threat Model API Workflows
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of sensitive business flows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Threat model these API workflows. Identify abuse cases, authorization boundaries, sensitive data exposure, rate-limit bypasses, and concrete mitigations.

output_examples.input: Review a user profile endpoint that accepts a user ID path parameter.
output: Checks whether the caller owns the requested profile or has an approved role.
Requires strict numeric validation for the path parameter.
Returns only safe profile fields and avoids password or token data.
Uses generic errors that do not expose database details.

output_examples.input: Design login protection for a public API.
output: Adds short-lived access tokens and revocable refresh tokens.
Uses slow password hashing and strong secret management.
Applies stricter rate limits to login and registration endpoints.
Logs suspicious authentication activity without recording credentials.

output_examples.input: Prepare a security checklist before release.
output: Confirms HTTPS, CORS restrictions, security headers, and safe error handling.
Reviews authorization on object and function-level operations.
Verifies request validation, output filtering, and dependency updates.
Documents monitoring, alerting, and incident response expectations.

best_practices: Validate authorization for every object and action, not only authentication status.
Use schema validation, parameterized data access, and safe output filtering together.
Apply rate limits, secure headers, structured logging, and generic production errors.
anti_patterns: Trusting client-supplied IDs, roles, or account ownership claims.
Building SQL or shell commands with untrusted request data.
Logging secrets, stack traces, tokens, or detailed database errors in responses.

faq.question: What API types does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers REST, GraphQL, and WebSocket API security concepts, with examples mainly written for Node.js and Express.

faq.question: Can it find vulnerabilities automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides review guidance and implementation patterns, but it does not run scanners or test live systems.

faq.question: Does it include OWASP guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It references OWASP API Security Top 10 categories and turns them into practical review areas.

faq.question: Is this useful for existing APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. You can use it to review routes, authentication flows, error handling, rate limits, and authorization checks.

faq.question: Does it handle secrets management?
faq.answer: It recommends environment-backed secrets, strong JWT keys, and avoiding hardcoded credentials in application code.

faq.question: Should this replace a penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It supports secure design and code review, but high-risk APIs still need expert testing.
